segment-text: based on the novel by Michael Connelly
EXT. CRIME SCENE (SILVERLAKE) - NIGHT

A media and spectator circus. Two cocky LAPD detectives, ARRANGO and WALTERS, watch as FBI profiler TERRY McCALEB strides past the police barricades. Wearing a blue FBI jacket, the hoary veteran ignores questions shouted out by the press. The words "Code Killer" feature prominently.
(disparaging) McCaleb... Whatever happens, it's his face on the front page.
As McCaleb reaches Arrango:
What do you got?
Fucking hemorrhoids. What are you gonna do about it?
Arrango's itching for trouble.
Victims five and six. killer strikes again.
Walters is a cooler head.
INT. TENEMENT - ENTRY HALLWAY - NIGHT
A dingy, dark place. Police work lights can't quite cut the gloom. McCaleb stops at the door with Walters and Arrango. The door is half off its hinges.
Neighbor came over to complain about the noise. Got run over at the door. Didn't see a thing.
We're calling him Chuck Taylor.
How's that?
Arrango points out a series of bloody sneaker prints on the floor. Headed for the door and away down the hall.
Converse hi-tops. Chuck Taylor. Get it?
A look and McCaleb continues in past them.
INT. LIVING ROOM - NIGHT
Not for the faint of heart. There are several darkened forms on the floor. Heavy, black plastic bags. An investigative team works.
We got the remains of two, maybe even three people.
Gives new meaning to the term gang bang.
Gruff, desensitized laughs from the cops. Walters motions McCaleb to a wall. There, written in blood: McCaleb, Catch me if you can. Arrango winks at his partner, then, to McCaleb:
Another love note. The two of you should get a room some place.
As McCaleb stares grimly...
EXT. CRIME SCENE (DOWNTOWN) - NIGHT
McCaleb exits. The crowd has doubled. A NEWS CREW bursts past the barricade. Police rush to block them, but to no avail. They nearly ring McCaleb.
Was another message left for you, Agent McCaleb!? -- Why do you think the killer has picked you?! -- What do the numbers mean?!

McCaleb scans the sea of humanity before him. Not exactly its best face. With a sigh, McCaleb starts to make his way through, then stops short. Ahead: FACE IN CROWD Seen for an instant. Wearing a hooded sweatshirt and low-billed cap. Staring at McCaleb, an odd look in his eye. A beat before McCaleb looks downward. THROUGH a thicket of legs, we spot his feet. Covering them are bloody Converse hi-tops. Chuck Taylor. McCaleb looks over, but the cops seem suddenly so far away. Hi-Top begins to drift back.
(toward cops) Hey... Hey! Over here!
Hi-Top turns and goes, suddenly swallowed up by the crowd.
(to no avail) He's over here!
No one listens. The Reporters continue to fire questions. Hi-Top is getting away. McCaleb fights his way through, charges down the street after him.
STREET
Running now, his face unclear as high-top looks back over his shoulder, sees that McCaleb is chasing him.
NARROW ALLEY

Hi-Top cuts down. McCaleb closes. At the end of the alley, an eight feet redwood fence. Hi-Top jumps, catches hold. As he pulls himself up, McCaleb hits the fence right behind him. The fence collapses. The two men sprawl to the ground. Hi-Top gets up, scoots away. McCaleb rolls to his feet; the chase is back on.
BACK YARDS

One after the next. Fences are scaled, kicked through. And McCaleb closes. All we hear are the FOOTFALLS, the RAGGED BREATHS of the two men. Then... a HEARTBEAT. McCaleb reaches for Hi-Top's shoulder, inches away now. A chain link fence looms ahead. Hi-Top leaps onto it, catches hold. As he pulls himself up, McCaleb is right behind. He catches hold of one of Hi-Top's legs. Hi-Top grips the top of the fence, rears back with his free leg and kicks McCaleb in the face. McCaleb drops. Hi-Top is up and over, dropping to the other side. alley behind some businesses.
An

McCaleb gets to his feet, one of Hi-Top's bloody sneakers in his hands. He starts to climb the fence. The HEARTBEAT INTENSIFIES. McCaleb pauses at the top of the fence, slides back down the way he came. He clutches at his left arm, a look of surprise on his face. The heartbeat eclipses all else. McCaleb is having a heart attack. He grips the fence to keep from falling. CHAIN LINK Suddenly, through the pain, McCaleb is aware of a form in the gloom. We don't see his face, but, fascinated, Hi-Top has come back to watch. McCaleb reaches into his jacket, seemingly for his chest. But when his hand comes back out, he's holding his .38 service revolver. Blinking against near blinding pain, he aims through the fence. Hi-Top runs for the dark as ROUND AFTER ROUND EXPLODES. The fourth sends him sprawling. The fifth and sixth sail wide as Hi-Top stands and staggers into blackness.

Suddenly illuminated by a spotlight. The revolver slips from his hand as the pain overwhelms him. Bleached white, he falls back into the dirt, his eyes wide. As he stares up at a silent, hovering LAPD helicopter, he mouths words we cannot understand. And as the HEARTBEAT BECOMES an ELECTRONIC BLIP... A grainy silver and black image of a heart: undulating and ghostly. The snaking line of a scope moves toward it. SUPERIMPOSE:
INT. CEDARS-SINAI HOSPITAL - EXAMINATION ROOM - DAY

On a gurney, his oddly-angled head in a brace, McCaleb closes his eyes as the line enters his heart. The voice belongs to DR. BONNIE FOX, the cardiologist who guides the line through an incision in McCaleb's neck.
Coming out, Terry. You did good.
Removing the scope, she tapes a gauze compress to his neck. As she unstraps the brace, McCaleb rubs his neck. He looks less than formidable in his hospital johnny.
Been taking your pills every day?
All thirty-four of them.
No fever at all?
No, I'm clean.
And no diarrhea?
I'm clean of that, too. Good.
Blood pressure and pulse?
Right on target.
And the boat? You're not working too hard on it?
I'm keeping it afloat. all.
Good. I'll see you in a bit. I want to check the blood work and get the tissue over to the lab.
As Dr. Fox heads out.
INT. CEDARS - HALLWAY - CARDIOLOGY UNIT - DAY

An orderly wheels McCaleb's gurney down the hall. they pass one room, McCaleb looks over. A young boy is on the bed, his body tied by tubes to a heart lung machine. A man in a suit, his father, sits at the foot of the bed. As his eyes drift out into the hall at McCaleb... McCaleb looks away.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - ON ISSUE OF BOAT RESTORATION
MAGAZINE McCaleb reads it in his hospital room. He looks up as Dr. Fox comes through the door, holding a lab report.
Don't start running laps yet, but I'm very pleased. No rejection, all the levels look good. I might lower your Prednisone in another week.
Good. I'm tired of shaving three times a day.
Dr. Fox smiles, warms a stethoscope with her breath.
Lean forward please. He leans forward. She pulls down his johnny, listens to his heart through his back.
Can I ask you something?
Don't talk. (a beat) Sit up.
She listens through his chest. For the first time we see it, the thirteen-inch white scar running over his heart like a rope. McCaleb half-grimaces at nothing in particular. Finally, she's done.
What did you want to ask
That boy in room 604. blood type?
What's his Dr. Fox studies him a beat.
It's different than yours if that's what you want to know.
How long has he been waiting?
About half as long as you did.
What are his chances?
Maybe fifty-fifty. Maybe less. What's bothering you, Terry?

(after a beat) When I was with the bureau we had to qualify on the range every year. You know, shoot targets. Circle around the heart scores more than the head. It's called the ten ring. Highest score.
Dr. Fox smiles sympathetically. Familiar ground to her.
Typical. You hang around almost two years waiting for a heart, draw your string out and nearly don't make it and now you wonder if we should've given it to you. You know what that is?
Bullshit?
Exactly. Go home and enjoy your boat. I'll see you next week.
EXT. CABRILLO MARINA (SAN PEDRO) - DAY

McCaleb walks across the main dock. He passes a forty-two-foot Hunter sailboat called the Double-Down. BUDDY NOONE plays harmonica in the cockpit. bathrobe, his hair standing up on one side. surfer, low maintenance live aboard. He wears a An aging
Morning, Terry.
It's two in the afternoon, Buddy. Whatever.
Hey, heads up, man.
What?
(gesturing) Somebody's on your boat.
Two boats down. The Following Sea. A woman stands by the sliding door leading to the boat's salon. McCaleb frowns.
Been here ten minutes. Looked harmless, I didn't say anything.
(starts forward) Thanks, Buddy.
The woman -- GRACIELLA RIVERS -- stares out across the water. She doesn't see McCaleb till he's stepping down aboard.
Can I help you?
She turns, a little startled.
I'm looking for Terrel McCaleb.
You found him.
I read about you in the paper, Mr. McCaleb. 'The Where Are They Now?' column. You know, in the Metro section?

Look, before you start your story, I have to tell you, you're not the first person to come out here and find me. (off no answer; she looks sad) I can recommend a good, private investigator. Someone who'll work hard and won't rip you off.
The article said you were good. It said you hated it whenever somebody got away.
If you read the article, then you also know what happened to me. I'm retired, Miss Rivers.
Graciella. I think you could help me. Maybe help yourself, too.
I don't need the money.
I'm not talking about money.

Something weird about how she said it. Graciella reaches into her purse, hands McCaleb a photo: a smiling woman watching a boy blow out seven candles on a birthday cake. The woman looks quite a bit like Graciella. He looks at it, looks back at her.
Your sister? Yes.
And her son.
Excuse me?
Which one is dead?
My sister. Gloria Torres. Her son's name is Raymond.
Glory. McCaleb holds the photo out, sighs.
Look, Miss Rivers, I just can't do this. I'm sorry.
She makes no move to take the photo.
Look at it again. Please. Just one more time and then I'll leave you alone. Tell me if you feel anything?
(irritated) I was an F.B.I. agent, not a psychic. Now do you want this photo back or not?
I have a double. You know, two for the price of one. You'll want to keep that one.
Why would I want to do that?
Graciella reaches out her hand. Past the photo McCaleb still holds out. She places the palm of her hand on his chest, runs it slowly down the front of his shirt, her fingers tracing the scar beneath.
Your heart. It was my sister's. (a beat) My phone number's on the back if you change your mind.
With that, Graciella steps up to the dock and starts away. Speechless, McCaleb watches her go, then turns over the photo. A number is scrawled on back. The mercury rises, stops at 98.6 degrees.
INT. THE FOLLOWING SEA - FORWARD HEAD - NIGHT

McCaleb takes the thermometer out of his mouth, reads it. He picks up a clipboard with a temperature chart. In a column he adds another in a series of dashes. No change. Looking at the mirror, he studies his eyes, then pulls off his shirt. Time to look at the scar. It's a ritual.
INT. THE FOLLOWING SEA - SALON - NIGHT

McCaleb sits at the desk, turns on a light. Hanging from the light, a Lucite block which holds his FBI badge. Tacked on the corkboard beyond that, a laminated newspaper line-up of code killer victims. Under them: 903 472 568. McCaleb frowns at it and turns away.
INT. THE FOLLOWING SEA - CABIN - NIGHT
McCaleb lies in his bunk staring up at the ceiling. Unable to sleep. Finally, he retrieves his watch from a slot on the wall. The glow-in-the-dark hands read 3:29
EXT. DOCK - PHONE BOOTH - NIGHT
A full moon shines down on the all but deserted sight. McCaleb dials the number on the back of the photo.

Miss Rivers, it's Terry McCaleb. I know it's late, but -(a beat) I'm going to go see the police tomorrow and ask about this. (another beat) Don't thank me. I don't want you to get your hopes up. I'm just going to take a look. I owe your sister that much. Now...
He takes a note pad and pencil from his pocket.
Before I start, I'll need some information.
EXT. LAPD WEST VALLEY DIVISION (RESEDA) - DAY
A cab pulls up out front and McCaleb gets out, a box of doughnuts in hand. As he heads for the entrance...
INT. DETECTIVE BUREAU - DAY
McCaleb steps to a counter. over. A YOUNG DETECTIVE steps
Can I help you?
Are Detectives Arrango or Walters in homicide? YOUNG DETECTIVE Your name?
McCaleb. Tell them it's about the Gloria Torres case. The young detective punches three digits into a phone,
whispers something as he looks McCaleb over. He hangs
Down the hall.
First door on your
INT. HOMICIDE BULLPEN - DAY
McCaleb has only gone a few steps when a solidly built man in a white shirt and tie steps in front of him. Gun in a shoulder harness. Detective Arrango from a few years ago.
Mr. McCaleb.
Detective Arrango. (CONTINUED)
McCaleb makes a point of shaking his hand.
I thought you had died, or at least retired. My guy up front said you're here about Glory Torres.
That's right. Is there a place where we can talk?
Talk about what?
I'm looking into her death.
Oh shit, here we go.
Irritated, Arrango turns, snaps his fingers to get the attention of his partner Walters who's on the phone. As Walters motions he'll be a minute...
You got five minutes before I toss you outta here.
INT. INTERVIEW ROOM - DAY
Arrango leads the way in, closes the door behind McCaleb.
Have a seat. My partner will be here in a minute.
(sitting down) Want a doughnut?
(also sitting) Keep your doughnuts. It's the sister, isn't it? You're working for the goddamn sister. Let me see your ticket.
I don't have a license, if that's what you mean.
Arrango glares at him a beat.
Jesus, it's stuffy in here.
Arrango gets up, adjusts a thermostat on the wall and goes back for his seat. As he does, McCaleb glances up at an air duct grill over the door.
You say you're conducting an
investigation into the Gloria
(sighs) Yes, that's right.
And you have no license in the state of California to operate as a private investigator, true?
True.
Walters enters. Without looking, Arrango signals him not to interrupt. Walters leans against the wall.
Do you understand, Mr. McCaleb, that it is a crime in this state to operate as a private investigator without a license?
Can we skip the bullshit and turn off the tape and camera and just talk a minute? Besides, Walters
is leaning against the microphone.
Arrango turns as Walters jumps away from the wall.
Why didn't you tell me?
Shuddup!
Have a doughnut, guys. to help.
How do you know Graciella Rivers?
She's an acquaintance. me to look into this.
She asked
McCaleb opens the box of doughnuts. Arrango, whose face is stone. Walters looks to
Well, I'm going to have one.
McCaleb takes out a cinnamon. After a beat, Walters grabs a glazed. Finally, against his better judgment, Arrango takes a powdered. They each chew a moment.

I don't know what the sister told you, Mr. Former-F.B.I.-profiler, but this isn't Charlie Manson or Ted fucking Bundy. It's a convenience store robbery. Some mope with a mask and a gun and the right ratio of balls to brains. That's all.
I know, but I told her I'd check it out. It's been two months. I thought maybe you guys wouldn't mind a fresh set of eyes.
Walters looks at Arrango, shrugs a why not.
I'm not here to show you up. Anything I come up with, you'll be the first to know.
What do you want?
Copy me the murder book and the video walk-through on the crime scene. I was always good with crime scenes.
Walters and Arrango exchange another look. Then...
I don't think so, man.
Let me ask you something, does the sister call you much?
All the time. Week in and week out I tell her the same thing. No
suspects, no leads.
This could get her off your case. If I see what you got and say you boys did your best, she might back off.
Arrango smiles at the thought of that.
You say you were good with crime scenes?
Yeah, if you made a tape...
Arrango winks at Walters, then looks back at McCaleb.
We got better than a crime scene tape. We got the crime. (stands) Come on. Bring those doughnuts with you.
INT. LIEUTENANT'S OFFICE - TELEVISION SCREEN - DAY

The screen flickers on. A black and white surveillance camera view of the counter of a convenience store. A date and time line runs across the bottom of the screen. The frame is empty a few seconds until the counterman leans in over the register. He breaks open a roll of quarters.
That's Kyungwon Kang, the owner.
He's spending his last few seconds on the planet here.
As he closes the register, a woman steps up. Easily recognizable from the photo -- Gloria Torres. Glory. She smiles as she sets a Hershey bar down on the counter.
Came in to buy a fucking candy bar. You believe that?
(softly) For her son...

As Gloria hands a dollar bill over, a man in a black ski mask and black jumpsuit moves up behind Gloria. She's still smiling as ski mask puts his right hand on her right shoulder and in one continuous move brings the muzzle of a handgun up to her left temple. Without hesitation, he pulls the trigger.

Badda-bing! McCaleb grimaces as bloody mist jettisons from the wound. Ski mask holds her up as a shield, then raises his left hand to FIRE a ROUND into Mr. Kang. Kang vainly grapples for a hold on the counter like a man going over a cliff. Ski mask lowers Gloria's body to the floor. drops out of sight. Her head Ski mask steps up as Mr. Kang pulls a revolver from under some papers bags. Ski mask shoots him in the face. Ski mask scoops the cash from the register, then picks up something off Mr. Kang's arm. He then reaches for something on the floor.
He picked up all the shells.
Ski mask looks directly at the camera and says something.
No sound on the camera, right?
Right. Whatever he said there, he said to himself.
Ski mask then makes one last pass on his way out.
You ain't gonna see that on 'America's Favorite Home Videos.'
(ignoring comment) Can I see it again? Slowed down?
It's not over yet.
What?
The good Samaritan comes in now.

On screen, a dark-haired, dark-complected man in jeans and a T-shirt enters. He hesitates, looks down at Gloria, then over the counter at Mr. Kang. He kneels over Gloria's body. Almost immediately, he's up and away.
He went down the aisles looking
Camera never got a good look at him. After he finished, he went outside, called 9-1-1 and split.
Voice on the tape was Latino. figure the guy was illegal. Afraid to stick around.
Too bad. He was there so soon, he probably saw the shooter's car, maybe the shooter.
We put out flyers, went on Channel 34. Nothing.
TV)

Arrango shrugs, annoyed. McCaleb rewinds, starts the tape again. This time in slow motion. As the gun gets pressed up against Gloria Torres' head, he freezes it. The smile has only halfleft her. Arrango smirks as McCaleb scribbles the time: McCaleb hesitates before resuming play.
Pause button's not gonna help her now.
McCaleb hits it. Gloria dies. The tape plays through ski mask saying something into the camera.
He winked.
What?

McCaleb rewinds, plays it slower. line, he definitely winks. As ski mask says his McCaleb freezes it again as the good Samaritan walks in. He notes the new time line: 22:42:55. As the tape finishes, McCaleb is visibly affected.
Okay, Mr. F.B.I. man, tell us something we don't know.
McCaleb stares over at Arrango, hardens up.

The shooter's been in the store before. He knew where the camera was. So he's either from the neighborhood or he cased the place. Plus this wasn't the first time. No hesitation, no panic, he picks up the brass. This guy's done it before.
Arrango and Walters exchange a look. Walters starts to say something, but Arrango holds up a hand to silence him.
What?
Neither man answers.
How many others? Neither man answers again.
Can I see the murder book?
Let us talk to the lieutenant and we'll get back to you.
Arrango and Walters both stand. Meeting over.
When?
Give me a number. We'll be in touch.

That's all he's getting. Taking out a pen, McCaleb writes a number on the top of the doughnut box. He shoves it in Arrango's hands, gives Walters a nasty look, then starts out with Arrango calling after him.
Hey, we don't need your shit, McCaleb, we got a whole plateful of our own!
EXT. SHERMAN MARKET (CANOGA PARK) - DAY
A cab pulls up and as McCaleb gets out...
Wait here.
McCaleb studies the storefront a beat. There's a pay phone right out front. A deep breath and he starts in.
INT. SHERMAN MARKET - DAY

McCaleb enters, smiling over at the elderly ASIAN WOMAN behind the counter. The counter where Mr. Kang died. She doesn't smile back. McCaleb goes to the display racks full of candy and picks out a Hershey bar. He steps to the counter, sets it down. As the Asian Woman RINGS it up, McCaleb looks up at the surveillance camera. He then hands the woman a dollar.
Are you Mrs. Kang? Yes.
I know you?
No, it's just... I heard what happened here. To your husband. I'm sorry. Yes, thank you. (a beat) The only way to keep evil out is to not unlock door. We can't do that. We must have business.

McCaleb nods that he understands. As she hands him his change, he looks one more time at the surveillance camera, then once behind him. Playing the crime over in his head. As he exits, he nods to Mrs. Kang.
Thank you.
EXT. SHERMAN MARKET - DAY
McCaleb steps to the pay phone. This is where the Good Samaritan stood. McCaleb reaches out, pretends to dial 9-1-1. A beat, then he scans the street.
McCALEB'S POV - STREET
A wide, busy four-lane boulevard. Across the street -- a strip mall parking lot. A man sitting in a car. He lifts a newspaper almost the instant McCaleb notices him.
Not alarmed. In fact, he's about to dismiss it, when...
Hey! You gonna pay me or should I drive you somewhere else?
McCaleb strides over to the cab, gets in.
The nearest public library.
The Cabbie thinks a beat, then starts to pull away. McCaleb looks across the street. The car is gone.
INT. LIBRARY - COMPUTER SCREEN - DAY

McCaleb is in the research section. On screen, he calls up the L.A. Times, accesses the search function. When it asks for key words, McCaleb types: "robbery, ski mask, shooting." He hits enter and waits. As the computer works, McCaleb dumps a handful of pills into the palm of his hand. As he chases them down with a bottle of water, the computer flashes a new screen. Two matches to the key words. McCaleb hits "print."
EXT. SHERIFF'S DEPARTMENT (WHITTIER) - DAY
The cab pulls up again.
INT. DETECTIVE WINSTON'S OFFICE (HOMICIDE) - DAY
The door opens. A DEPUTY shows McCaleb in.
Detective Winston said to wait here. She'll be right with you. The Deputy leaves McCaleb alone. McCaleb steps over to a wall of citations and a

smattering of press clippings. One is from a police It shows both McCaleb and Det. Jaye Winston receiving citations for an arrest of "The Cemetery Man." The door opens and JAYE enters, looks happy to see him.
How's it going, Terry? you got a new ticker. I heard
(as they shake hands) Yeah, and I'm doing okay. You? Same old same old. What's up? You on a private ticket now?
No, just doing a favor for a friend. What case is it?
McCaleb pulls the LA Times library printouts from his bag.
James Cordell. A.T.M. holdup on January twenty-two. Newspaper says he was shot by a guy wearing a ski mask. Who's the friend you're doing the favor for?
Graciella Rivers. Her sister was shot in a convenience store holdup two weeks ago. Guy with a ski mask. An odd beat.
So did I get it right? connected?
Are they
Same guy, Terry. Out of your orbit, though. Just a scumbag with a gun. Three-strike shit, that's all.
What do you mean?
You know, guys with two felony convictions. A third puts them away for life. (MORE)
So instead of leaving witnesses, they kill 'em. Robbery, murder, it's the same sentence either way.
I saw the boys in L.A. They showed me the tape, but wouldn't copy me the book. I bet they copied you. (a beat) You owe me one, Jaye.
Don't you think I know that?
James Cordell, 35, as seen by an ATM camera. He slides his card into the cash machine. He hesitates, looks back over his right shoulder, tracking something behind him.
Shooter's back there, but I'm betting the mask isn't on yet.
Otherwise Cordell would've run. Cordell turns back to the machine, waits. Suddenly, the gun comes into frame, just kissing Cordell's left temple. Jaye freeze-frames.
Same

She hits play and James Cordell's brains are blown out. He falls away to reveal the same shooter. Ski mask and black jumpsuit aside, it's him. As he reaches for the cash in the slot, he mouths something. McCaleb freezes it.
He said something in the Valley tape, also. (shrugs) Guy's a chatterbox.
Have you brought in a lip reader?
Jaye looks at him for a beat, feels foolish that she hasn't.
He took the money from the machine not the victim. So?
He took it from the bank. it a federal offense.

Makes Jaye shrugs, hits play. Ski mask picks up the shell casing and goes. Jaye fast forwards. After a minute, she hits play. A man, James Lockridge, steps up, crouches by Cordell's body. Lockridge is stocky, bald across the top. James Lockridge. He pulled into
Cordell died on the way to the
hospital.
You got any leads at all?

Been following the three-strikes thing. Looking up local stick-up men with two or more falls under their belts. I think the man in the ski mask is one of them. Used to be a robber, now he's a robbing murderer.
What about the gun? Heckler and Koch. Expensive for a holdup man and weird that he'd hang onto it after the first killing.
I figured he stole it, but that didn't lead anywhere. I hate to say it, McCaleb, but I think we're going to need fresh blood to solve this one.
Can you copy the murder books, Jaye? Yours and L.A.'s?
I'll ask the captain, but I don't see why not. He was the lieutenant on the Cemetery Man case.
Glad someone got promoted.
INT. VALLEY COMPUTING - DAY
JAMES LOCKERIDGE sits at his cubicle typing into a computer. A bit of a nerd, he looks up as the office manager leads McCaleb over.
(extending hand) Mr. Lockeridge, I'm Terry McCaleb. Thanks for seeing me.
Lockridge looks from the hand to McCaleb.
Are you a policeman?
F.B.I. Retired. I'm investigating a related case for another victim's family. Good.
Police are stupid.
Why do you say that?
Didn't they tell you?
McCaleb looks at him, shrugs.

Why would they? When I found the guy shot, I called 9-1-1. They got the address transposed and they sent the ambulance to the wrong place. It took twenty minutes. Guy was breathing the whole time. He might've made it if they hadn't fucked up.
These things happen, I guess.
No. They fucked up and the guy died. I'm real busy. I agreed to see you because you were already here. What do you want?
I read your statement, but I'd like to hear from you what happened.
I needed cash. As I pulled into the lot, a guy driving out almost hit me. It was dark. All I saw was it was a car. No plates, no
face, nothing. I walk up to the
minutes later, they went driving
Did you notice anything at all, detail-wise?
Yeah. That it could've been me. It could've been him finding me. With that ambulance driving right on by!
EXT. ATM - READYTELLER (LANCASTER) - DAY

A lonely spot even during the day. McCaleb steps up. He stands there a beat, looks down at the ground where the bloodstain still isn't quite gone. McCaleb inserts his ATM card, looks back over his shoulder, then up and down the street. He closes his eyes, replaying the crime in his mind. Suddenly, a hand reaches up toward McCaleb's shoulder! Someone's behind him. As the hand comes down... McCaleb wheels to face a longhaired MAN wearing Wayfarer sunglasses and a single, dangling crucifix earring.
You done?
McCaleb blinks in surprise, then gathers his wits. Grunting that he is, he takes his ATM card from the slot and walks away. The man watches after him a beat, then turns to the machine.
EXT. DOCK (SAN PEDRO) - SUNSET
A taxi pulls up and lets McCaleb out. He carries a stack of files and videotapes. He looks tired. Buddy raises a beer from the deck of the Double Down.
Here's to you, Terry. McCaleb nods, continues past, then turns, looks at Buddy.
What do you got going tomorrow?
Same as always. A big fat goose egg.
I need a driver for a few days. Ten bucks an hour plus meals.
(perking up) What is it, an investigation?
I'm looking for a driver, Buddy, not a partner. Okay.
Whose car.
We take yours, I pay for gas. take mine, I sit in the back.
It's got air bags up front.
Right, cuz of your chest, right?
McCaleb nods, no patience left for the day.
